calendar.matters:
NOTICE OF HEARING AND TEMPORARY RESTRAINING ORDER (ELDER OR DEPENDENT ADULT ABUSE)
Off Calendar Sep-09-2009 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: This cause came on regularly as scheduled for Elder Abuse/Dependent Adult TRO hearing this day.

Dept. 204, Judge Pro Temp: Sue Kaplan, Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda, Reporter: Helen Sorep-Matley, CSR 225, Bailiff: Deputy J. Capozzoli.

The parties were sworn.

Richard Grabstein, Esq. for and with the Petitioner/Conservatee: Sidney Shaw.
Rowena Navia, Esq. for and with the Conservator of the Person: Felipe Rubio.
Andrew Schultz, Esq. for and with Conservator of the Estate: Scott Hodges.

Respondent: John Zapata was present in pro per.
Witness for the Respondent: Alton Phillips.

The Court stated that there was a Domestic Violence order in Shaw vs. Zapata (case no. FDV-09-806645), that on February 11, 2009 Judge Mahoney had granted a five year restraining order in favor of Mr. Shaw against Mr. Zapata, that Mr. Shaw had filed a motion to dismiss the DV restraining order which was taken off calendar in April 2009, and that Mr. Shaw recently filed another motion to dismiss in the DV case which is set for October 1, 2009 in the Family Law Department. In addition, that Court stated that there was currently an operative order; the DV restraining order and had it known that the DV order was in place the temp in this case would not have been signed. The Court expressed concerns as to who should have brought the Elder Abuse action and stated that it would take this matter off calendar because the motion in the DV restraining order should take place first. The Court indicated that if the DV restraining order is terminated and the parties proceed with the Elder Abuse action it would be ok with the Court. Ms. Fox addressed her client being able to act independently since he is now under conservatorship. Ms. Navia asked the Court if Mr. Zapata was still under a restraining order and the Court responded yes. Ms. Navia also asked if the restraining order needed to be enforced would her client be the person to do so. The Court responded yes.
